# 

# 1\. Identifying and Defining {#1.-identifying-and-defining}

## 1.1 Problem Statement {#1.1-problem-statement}

In a world filled with online distractions in the form of social media and free entertainment, staying productive serves to be a difficult task for many juvenile students. This is a significant issue – those who are in adolescence today are the same cohort that will operate the world in the future, hence it is important to develop crucial skills such as organisation and planning early so this does not hinder them later on. This is why a study app is necessary in ensuring that students are able to stay on task while keeping their study materials organised. 

---

## 1.2 Project Purpose and Boundaries {#1.2-project-purpose-and-boundaries}

The purpose of this project is to create a functional study application with functionality and customisability. The ideal end goal is to create a polished application that can accommodate all the study tools the client (me) needs, whether that be a calendar for time management or a note organiser for organising notes. This enables many apps to be compressed into one.

However, it is important to remember that no matter how polished the final result is, the problem still remains in students’ unwillingness to study, this app is more like a potential fix for one aspect of the problem and not the cure.

---

## 1.3 Stakeholder Requirements {#1.3-stakeholder-requirements}

The client requires the app to have, at the very minimum:

- A Graphical User Interface (GUI)

  - A login page (may require HTML, CSS and JavaScript)

  - Task Creator

  - A ‘Settings’ Page

This provides basic functionality for not only students, but also other working class individuals due to the ability to organise tasks.

The client also wishes users to feel a desire to experience the application. This makes studying more engaging and attractive.

---

## 1.4 Functional Requirements {#1.4-functional-requirements}

The application must enable the user to:

- Take notes

- Revise flashcards

- Set timers

- Create and complete checklists

- Organise their study materials

The application must also have the ability to delete the student’s study data after confirmation, in compliance with data minimisation.

The study app will need to have the capability to:

- Create local folders

- Access local files

- Modify local files

It would be beneficial if the study app had the ability to format files suitably, but this is not absolutely necessary.

---

## 1.5 Non-Functional Requirements {#1.5-non-functional-requirements}

Performance:

- The app must run smoothly without consuming many resources. This is important as a study app is likely to be used on battery. This ensures the study app does not severely impact the battery life of their machine, allowing the user to study for longer periods of time.

Usability:

- The app icon must be simple, yet aesthetically pleasing, but not too distracting.

- The app must use universally recognised icons. This prevents text from becoming the means of navigation, helping the user avoid initial confusion between their flashcards and the settings for example.

- Accessibility options for text size must be included. This ensures that the user is able to study effectively: it would be counterproductive to study from text that is unreadable.

Security:

- The app must have a login page at the minimum. It will have a login page every time it is opened throughout the development phase, but will only require one login after release. This ensures the application is secure while also maintaining convenience for the user.

Reliability:

- The app must almost never crash. This ensures that the user is able to reliably study without needing to worry about all their files and notes vanishing due to subpar stability of the application.

---

## 1.6 Constraints {#1.6-constraints}

Some constraints for this project include:

- Time: Time, specifically the time management of the developer (me) is an issue as poor time management will induce stress within the last few days before the deadline.

- Technical Knowledge: Although the developer has some experience with TKinter and TTKBoostrap, they are still not as familiar with the two as they would like to be. This means that the developer will be constantly learning throughout the development process, opening options for exploration and experimentation. Normally, these options are beneficial, however, broadening the scope of possibilities is a hindrance to the developer’s time management.

---

## 1.7 Requirements Analysis and Prioritisation {#1.7-requirements-analysis-and-prioritisation}

Since the client has required a minimum of a login page, settings page and task creator, these will be prioritised. The client has also requested a GUI, which will be made into a prerequisite for these requirements. In alignment with the problem statement, the client has provided an overall suitable proposal, and they have mentioned that this project cannot be the full cure. This means the client has sensibly thought over the idea of the study application, specifically, considering the real world as a hidden constraint. However, due to time management weaknesses and technical knowledge constraints, the client still may not be able to receive the ‘solution’ they require. If that is the case, it will be solely due to the developer’s tendency to veer off course, exploring different ‘rabbit holes’ instead of focusing on the requested application.

---

# 2\. Research and Planning {#2.-research-and-planning}

## 2.1 Development Methodology {#2.1-development-methodology}

The chosen development approach for this project will be WAgile. The WAgile model involves strict planning combined with a flexible development cycle that combines the adaptability of the Agile approach with the strictness of the Waterfall model. This means the less frequent updates allows the developer to add personal touches to the application, thus enhancing their user experience. This also enables the developer (i.e. me) to do less sprints, so that I do not have to constantly have to worry about launching my program at the end of the sprint. Furthermore, this application does not have a high stakes implementation, so using waterfall methodology would only lead to more stress as the deadline for the program nears.

---

## 2.2 Tools and Technologies {#2.2-tools-and-technologies}

Tools:

- Visual Studio Code: Visual Studio Code (VSC) is a code editor with a user friendly interface. This enables the developer to output work, ensuring that the developer is not as stressed towards the deadline.

- GitHub Desktop: GitHub and GitHub Desktop will be used as it ensures the developer can easily track their changes, which can be more easily documented during the development phase. This is done via commits, so the developer will only need to worry about pushing their changes to GitHub.

- School PC: School PCs will be used as they are provided by the school for educational purposes. This project falls under educational purposes, so School PCs will be used.

Programming Languages:

- Python: Python is a beginner friendly programming language that enables the developer to access many libraries while also containing the ability to collaborate with other developers who also program in Python.

Libraries:

- TKinter: TKinter enables the developer to create a smooth and efficient Graphical User Interface. (May end up using TTKBootstrap)

- TTKBootstrap: Using TTKBootstrap gives the developer access to the theme selection that comes with TTKBootstrap while also still maintaining the performance of TKinter.

- Random: Random provides aspects of the study app with functionality, such as shuffling flashcards. This is crucial so that the user does not end up memorising the answers to the flashcards in a set order, which would negatively affect academic performance.

- Datetime: Datetime is used for providing a sleek, minimalist interface when the user opens the study application.

---

## 2.3 Gantt Chart / Timeline {#2.3-gantt-chart-/-timeline}

![][image2]

Link: [https://canva.link/0fj3fuo7jpbl3xm](https://canva.link/0fj3fuo7jpbl3xm) 

Summary: Overall, time was allocated in a rather staggered manner in order to ease the transition between different phases of the project while also hopefully ensuring that most of the work is done in the last few days before the deadline. One will notice that the evaluation stages have less time allocated, this is simply due to the developer wishing to maximise the development time to produce a quality application.

---

## 2.4 Communication Plan {#2.4-communication-plan}

For this project, the client is myself. This means feedback may be extremely biased depending on the amount of effort put into development. 

For feedback, the client will ask their friends to test, rate and review the application. The aim is to receive feedback as if it were from an anonymous developer, thus removing any biases. 

In the end, feedback was not obtained as the developer simply did not manage their time optimally, thus leading to an application that was purely based on biased client feedback. This was not a large dilemma, just a little less balanced than it would have been had the program received outside feedback.

---

## 2.5 Resource Allocation Justification {#2.5-resource-allocation-justification}

Resources allocation will be flexible, the reason for this is due to potentially following a more flexible approach to the development process. 

Resources will be allocated as the client and developer see fit, for example:

- Time: The developer aims to strictly follow the time plan as per the Gantt Chart. This minimises stress buildup and ensures that a satisfactory result is provided to the client.

- Software and Hardware tools:

  - Software tools: Software tools such as code editors will be used for the development of the task. This ensures a simple, comfortable and user friendly environment for the creation of output (i.e. for the developer to do work). Version control tools, specifically GitHub and GitHub Desktop will be used in order to provide a simple user interface and minimise developer confusion. This keeps the developer on track, minimising the chances that the developer can manage their time poorly.

  - Hardware tools: Hardware tools such as school infrastructure (School PCs will be used in order for the developer to save the battery of their own machine for other school tasks; the creation of the study application should not hinder the developer’s study).

- Human Input: Human input such as feedback from peers will likely occur throughout the development process, with peers and the client’s trusted friends providing more feedback towards the end. For teacher feedback, the developer and client will need to exercise their time management skills in order to obtain feedback of a higher order.

---

# 3\. System Design {#3.-system-design}

This section justifies the use of modelling tools to represent system structure, data flow, and processing logic prior to implementation.

---

## 3.3 Structure Chart {#3.3-structure-chart}

Excalidraw link: [https://excalidraw.com/\#json=PejtutoX49\_x0rPAqqmtZ,dHuB8h-Hv2wLwL5bFfaLrQ](https://excalidraw.com/#json=PejtutoX49_x0rPAqqmtZ,dHuB8h-Hv2wLwL5bFfaLrQ) 

![][image5]

---

## 3.4 IPO Chart {#3.4-ipo-chart}

| Inputs | Processing | Outputs |
| :---- | :---- | :---- |
| User Inputs | The user’s inputs such as typing will be registered and written to the file using read and write commands. | Main study app updates to show their modifications. |
| Login Inputs (Username and Password) | Input validation, input sanitisation and error handling will be used for the creation of usernames and passwords. | Main study app offers functionality to user after login |
| Events (Create files, delete files) | File handling such as storing files in .txt format will be implemented. | Pop up window so the user can decide where to put files. |
| File Paths (Documents) | All files should be saved locally to the user’s ‘Documents’ folder. | The file is saved and the user can modify it. |

---

## 3.5 Data Dictionary {#3.5-data-dictionary}

| Name | Type | Format | Size | Description | Example | Constraints |
| :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- |
| created | boolean | True/False | 1 | Determines whether something was created | True | N/A |
| deleted | boolean | True/False | 1 | Determines whether something was deleted | False | N/A |
| user | string | XX…XX | 15 | User’s username | USER | Alphanumeric & more than 2 characters |
| password (unencrypted) | string | XXX123\#\#\# | 15 | User’s unencrypted password | ABC123\!\!\! | Minimum 8 Characters & Not Alphanumeric |
| valid\_usr | boolean | True/False | 1 | Flags username as valid or invalid | True | N/A |
| valid\_pwd | boolean | True/False | 1 | Flags password as valid or invalid | False | N/A |
| error\_msg | string | XXX123\#\#\# | 25 | Error message for password | Password must not be alphanumeric | N/A |
| greeting | string | XXX123\#\#\# | 25 | Greeting for when user logs in | Good morning, User | Affected by hour and user |
| hour | int | 12 | 6 | Hour of day | 17 | 1-24 |

## ---

## 3.6 UML Class Diagram (if OOP) {#3.6-uml-class-diagram-(if-oop)}

Excalidraw Link: [https://excalidraw.com/\#json=RIehb8qi\_ToLwm4PWOlNt,QyziXTf5DYe2VQ7HrbtWYQ](https://excalidraw.com/#json=RIehb8qi_ToLwm4PWOlNt,QyziXTf5DYe2VQ7HrbtWYQ) 

![][image6]

Most of the classes in my program are CTkFrames, meaning that there will only ever be one class visible at a time. 

---

# 4\. Producing and Implementing {#4.-producing-and-implementing}

## 4.1 Development Process {#4.1-development-process}

Originally, I had planned to use a WAgile Approach due to the fact that this type of program would require frequent updates, but I ended up leaning more waterfall style approach, as I ended up forgetting about my assigned sprints.

Engineering techniques I used when developing Locked In(c). included a modular design, an Object Oriented Programming (OOP) approach, the reuse of code from different classes, and Input Validation and Error Handling. A modular approach, while more difficult for me to implement, given my first attempt at this approach contained one line within the main module, broadened the possibilities while keeping everything clean and organised. An OOP approach enabled me to easily call upon different classes in different modules, which meant that I would easily be able to reuse my code, keeping the program overall more efficient, requiring fewer lines and less repeats. Input Validation and Error Handling were also baked into the Login and Register Screens, however, due to time constraints, will likely be built into the functions in a future update.

Overall, even though I had planned to use an agile approach and intended to implement error handling throughout the program, less than optimal time management led project development astray.

---

## 4.2 Key Features Developed {#4.2-key-features-developed}

The core features of the system are:

- **Note Taker:** This was included as more of an extra.  
- **Calendar:** This feature was a necessary feature to include, as it enables the user to stay organised within the study application. This also addresses the problem statement, ensuring the user stays organised as they should.  
- **To Do Tracker:** Again, this feature is necessary to include, as it ensures the user stays organised and has a physical list of what they need to do so that they are not required to remember everything mentally which can be both stressful and exhausting (may or may not be speaking from experience).  
- **Flashcard Maker:** This feature is necessary to include because it allows the user to revise their content through extremely short spaced repetitions. This aligns with the problem statement, it is a study app after all.  
- **A ‘Settings’ Page:** This feature was included for accessibility reasons. The options to change the theme to light mode and set custom accessibility text sizes enables this application to be used by those who have impaired vision with no issues.

---

## 4.2.1 Back-End Engineering Contribution {#4.2.1-back-end-engineering-contribution}

Backend engineering contributed drastically to the success of the software, from the password encryption to the file handling. 

The backend was responsible for the whole functionality of the program, such as the creation of notes, and the input validation within the login and registration screens.

---

## 4.3 Screenshots of Interface {#4.3-screenshots-of-interface}

Include annotated screenshots explaining how the user interacts with the system.

| ![][image7] Title Screen: When the user opens the application, they are greeted with a minimalistic title screen. | ![][image8] Login Page: When the user clicks the login button, they are directed to the login screen. Users must login with existing accounts. |
| :---- | :---- |
| ![][image9] **Registration Screen:** When the user clicks the register button, they are directed to the registration screen, where they can create an account. | ![][image10] **Welcome Screen:** Once the user logs in, they are greeted by a minimalistic clock and welcome screen. The home button takes them to the next page. |
| ![][image11] **Home Screen/Main Page:** When the user clicks the home button on the welcome screen, they are greeted with the feature widgets. | ![][image12] **To-Do/Task Tracker:** When the user clicks the leftmost to-do widget/card, they are taken to the to-do screen, where they can add tasks that they need to complete. |
| ![][image13] **Note Taker:** When the user clicks on the notes card, they are taken to the notes section, where they can create and type notes. | ![][image14] **Flashcards:** When the user clicks on the flashcards tile, they are taken to a section where they can create and interact with flashcards. |
| ![][image15] **Calendar:** When the user clicks on the calendar card, they are taken to the calendar, which allows them to set events for the future. These have specific dates, hence the separation from the to-do function. | ![][image16] **Settings:** When the user clicks on the icon in the top right corner, they are greeted with a basic settings page, where they can change appearance and text size. This is also where they can delete their data, with the ‘Delete All Data’ button. |
| ![][image17] **Data Deletion:** When the user clicks on ‘Delete All Data’, they are shown this prompt, asking to confirm the data deletion. | Note: Pressing the ‘X’ button in their operating system closes the window, hence no exit button is included. |

---

## 4.4 Version Control Summary (Optional) {#4.4-version-control-summary-(optional)}

**Summarise** commits, iterations, or sprints if version control was used.

Overall, my commits mostly consisted of development of new features, or improving existing features, with a few small details that I had forgotten scattered through the days.

---

# 5\. Testing and Evaluation {#5.-testing-and-evaluation}

## 5.1 Testing Methods Used {#5.1-testing-methods-used}

There are several testing approaches that I could have used to test my program, these being, Unit Testing, User Testing and Boundary Testing. 

Unit testing is when a single unit of the whole program is tested to ensure that it is not the issue in the case of a program error.

User Testing is when the user evaluates usability, trying to discover errors that the developer may not have addressed.

Boundary Testing involves testing boundary values for each input field. For example, if the requirement for a password was for it to have 8 or more characters, I would test 7 characters and then 8 characters and observe the outputs.

The main method of testing used to debug my program was user testing, where I would navigate to the part which needed to be tested. This was used to improve security as it enabled me to develop a system with multiple user accounts and hashed passwords following the instructions of Large Language Models like ChatGPT (which I often used for debugging) and Claude (which I often looked to for instructions where there was something that I did not know how to do). For example, while using ChatGPT to debug, I found that my username variable was not as secure as it could have been (it was stored as a global variable), so I followed its recommendation to change it to self.username instead. This version did not break encapsulation, thus keeping the program secure by following OOP Principles.

---

## 5.2 Test Cases and Results {#5.2-test-cases-and-results}

| Test ID | Description | Expected Result | Actual Result | Pass/Fail |
| :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- |
| TC01 | User Test (Valid user) | No Error Message | No Error Message | Pass |
| TC02 | User Test (Invalid user) | Error Message | Error Message | Pass |
| TC03 | User Test (Valid password) | No Error Message | No Error Message | Pass |
| TC04 | User Test (Invalid password) | Error Message | Error Message | Pass |
| TC05 | User Test (Existing User) | No Error Message  (on Login Screen) | No Error Message (on Login Screen) | Pass |
| TC06 | User Test (Existing User) | Error Message (on Registration Screen) | Error Message (on Registration Screen) | Pass |
| TC07 | User Test (Non-Existing User) | No Error Message (on Registration Screen) | No Error Message(on Registration Screen) | Pass |
| TC08 | User Test (Non-Existing User) | Error Message (on Login Screen) | Error Message (on Login Screen) | Pass |
| TC09 | Unit Test (Password hash) | Password hash generated | Password hash generated | Pass |

---

## 5.3 Evaluation Against Requirements {#5.3-evaluation-against-requirements}

Overall, the program meets most of the requirements set by the client, meeting all their minimum requirements as of the present. 

One identified requirement was the ability to take notes. This requirement has been met, hence the notes function within the app (it is the second of the four main functions). Other requirements that have also been fully met are the ability to revise flashcards, and the inclusion of a settings page.

Similar requirements such as creating and completing checklists and organising study materials were completed to some degree, however, not to the full extent that the client had imagined. For example, the ability to create multiple checklists was not implemented resulting in the requirement not being met. Also, study materials such as notes are not able to be organised apart from the gallery view, so without specific folder based organisation, the requirement could not be met. Another requirement that was partially met was the ability to create local folders. All file paths within the program are relative paths, so I was able to create local folders, to some degree. However, the idea was to create local folders on main folders such as the Desktop or the Documents folders, which was not met.

There were also other requirements such as setting timers which were simply not met at all. Even though this would not have been too difficult to implement, the developer simply ran out of time to implement it. This was due to their poor time management.

Despite all these requirements that have not been fully satisfied, the developer has still exceeded the minimum requirements of the application, and thus, the client is overall not too disappointed, though not extremely satisfied either.

---

## 5.4 Improvements and Future Development {#5.4-improvements-and-future-development}

Overall, some limitations to the development of this project were time, time management and development skills. 

In future updates, the developer aims to implement the remaining requirements, which would enhance the program’s usability. For example, the developer is planning to implement the timer feature in the future, perhaps as part of the initial update. This is realistic – the developer has just enough time to implement this, while also having enough skill too, plus it also considers any upcoming events that the developer may have.

---

## 5.5 Evaluation of Social, Legal and Communication Issues

**Social Issues:**  
There are not many social issues that this application currently presents. This is because this application would not currently be considered ‘good enough’ for many people, with the demand in requirements and seamless integration growing increasingly higher. Many people, with limited knowledge of programming would complain about how this application doesn’t have Feature A or Feature B, which would lead to declining user satisfaction overall.

**Ethical Issues:**  
There are no ethical issues in relation to this application. This application was developed of free will, and the developer did not require any reward for their labour. Plus, the client and the developer are the same person, thus the developer could not raise any ethical concerns anyways.

**Legal Issues:**  
In its current state, this application does not pose any threat to more mainstream organisational apps such as Notion or Obsidian, which means it would not raise any infringement issues (even though it hopefully would not raise any of these issues anyways). As disappointing as it might be, this application may not even be used by the client after the submission of this task, thus it would likely remain forgotten, not raising any legal issues.

---

# 6\. Feedback, Security and Reflection {#6.-feedback,-security-and-reflection}

## 6.1 Summary of Client or Peer Feedback {#6.1-summary-of-client-or-peer-feedback}

|  | Feedback (Various forms) |
| :---- | :---- |
| **Levin \- Summary** | Victor’s app is very special. It uses customtkinter to build a very modern, sleek and stylish app. The tools are very functional and userful, and respond to user demands almost instantly. However, he could improve his app by adding Windows usability. Overall a good app by Victor. |
| **Ronen** | Plus:  Clean, very impressive UI, Smooth Transitions, good functionalities Minus:  Could use better error handling and greater functionality to differentiate it from a traditional study app with calendar, flashcards, etc Implication:  Overall, a clean, organised application with a good approach in fulfilling the trope of a traditional study app, which could use further functionality to differentiate it from other implementations. |
| **Alex \- PMI Evaluation** | Plus: Good input validation Clean UI Really good features, works well Minus: Game should start on home page UI looks a bit empty Doesn’t save notes when clicking home page Implication: Cool study app with good features, however the UI could be designed more intricately (visually appealing) |
| **Shawn \- PMI Evaluation** | Plus: Clean UI with nice colours Lots of cool features Good security and login page Minus: Instead of starting on the time page when logging in, start on the homepage Spacing could be better (for UI) Notes don’t autosave Implication Interesting study app with lots of useful features, small bugs here and there, UI could also be improved, but overall very nice.  |

Feedback was rarely collected throughout development, even though it was intended. Again this came down to time management. 

However, friends constantly suggested improvements towards the interface, such as the need for a light mode and the ability to change text sizes. This feedback was taken into serious consideration, and, as the settings page of the program suggests, was also implemented.

Overall, in regards to the client, they are extremely satisfied with the final outcome, even though some features could be improved in order to support multiple users. Since the client was myself, I shall take the peer feedback into as much consideration as my own self regulations.

Note: I have since fixed the saving feature that Alex and Shawn have mentioned.

---

## 6.2 Secure Software Design and Data Handling {#6.2-secure-software-design-and-data-handling}

Throughout the development of the application, I have managed to safely and securely, collect, use, and store data. This was done by only collecting necessary data, enabling data deletion for all users, and validating inputs before authenticating the user. In addition to this, I have used some secure coding practices, including Input Validation, Authentication and Basic Encryption. 

For example, I have hashed all passwords, thus ensuring user security, as there is no method to obtain the raw password without decoding the hash. I also stored files somewhat safely within the application, ensuring that file folders were not visible at the surface level (they were one folder down in the ‘files’ folder).

I have also maintained data integrity, enabling users to delete all their personal files, which may have contained confidential personal information. However, the user is never able to delete their account, thus this does not maintain data integrity. This could result in a loss of user trust, as some of their details would still be in the application after they have supposedly deleted ‘all of their data’.

Input validation and error handling was necessary to implement within the login and registration screens. This ensured the safety of the application, preventing malicious code injection.

For data storage, I used .json files under self-categorised folders. This ensured that all data was organised and accessible in the case the user required to manually delete their data. However, this does grant me access to other user’s data, so this is not entirely secure, if at all.

However, I have still missed some key areas, for instance, files are accessible by all users. This means I need an authorisation system in order to only give each user the permission to access their own files, however, I could not figure this out during the time of development, so I will need to implement this in a future update. I also needed to implement session management, however, this was also not completed due to my poor time management, which caused time control issues for the task as a whole.

Overall, the undertaken approach to safely collect, use, and store data was to build a basic input validation system that encrypted user passwords, which is one of the most important aspects of the user profile, other than sensitive personal information.

---

## 6.3 Personal Reflection {#6.3-personal-reflection}

Throughout this project, I have been constantly learning, from learning a new library, to learning file handling in python. 

Some challenges that I encountered were the initial creation of the GUI, for I had not created one in such a long time, hence I had to re-learn how to create one. 

Most of these challenges were overcome with the guidance of Artificial Intelligence Models such as ChatGPT and Claude. These were used for debugging purposes and in aspects where I desperately needed help. For example, for file handling, I originally watched some YouTube tutorials by BroCode, and tried to implement what I learned into my code, however, the code did not work, so I used ChatGPT to debug and identify errors. It also suggested improvements to the program at the time, teaching me exactly how to fix my issues and enhance usability of my program.

Claude was also used to instruct me if I did not know how to do something, which was quite often, including walking me through the for loops, which is one of my weak points.

Overall, even though most of my solutions were handed to me on a silver spoon, I did end up learning plenty as part of the development of this application. 

---
